assessments.rationale: All three Marquardt positions use water-based media. Glycol/water mixtures (30-35% propylene glycol) explicitly supported. CIP uses dilute aqueous solutions. Water lines are the ideal medium.
Glycol: -5 to +5C. CIP caustic peak: 75-85C (within 90C limit; short 15-30 min cycles). Water/HLT: up to 85C. Sensor not in brewhouse wort path (100C).
Glycol 2-4 bar, CIP 2-4 bar, water supply 3-6 bar, BBT 0.5-2 bar. No brewery circuit approaches 10 bar.
All target circuits are closed, pressurized loops with negligible chronic air entrainment. Sensor anomaly detection provides diagnostic value during commissioning.
Glycol circuits have stable temperatures. CIP has step transitions but built-in NTC compensation reduces error to 0.2%, adequate for flow verification.

CIP circuits expose wetted parts to 2-3% NaOH at 75-85C and 1% phosphoric acid at 50-60C. Polymer housing designed for water/glycol needs material verification for repeated hot-caustic cycles. Does not affect primary glycol position.
assessments.mitigation: Verify housing polymer (PP/PPS/PEEK) compatibility with dilute caustic (2-3% NaOH) at 85C via immersion testing; Deploy CIP sensor on return line where temperature and concentration are lower; Limit initial market entry to glycol-only position, deferring CIP deployment

assessments.rationale: Glycol: 10-50 l/min per zone. CIP: 50-200 l/min per circuit. Water: >5 l/min. All well above DN20/DN25 detection thresholds (4.2 l/min).
Glycol and water circuits are pressurized closed loops (always full). CIP sensor reads during active cleaning cycles when pipes are full. Empty-pipe detection flags fault state.
Compact craft brewery glycol manifolds may have limited straight runs at distribution branch points. Large/mid-size breweries have adequate layout. Standard flow conditioners solve the issue.
assessments.mitigation: Install after glycol manifold header or at chiller outlet where straight runs are available; Add inline vane-type flow conditioner ($50-100) at constrained locations; Specify minimum installation clearance in OEM integration documentation

assessments.rationale: Ultrasonic ($200-600) costs more upfront than paddle-wheel ($50-200) but delivers zero maintenance in glycol service where mechanical sensors suffer bearing wear. ROI positive within 12-18 months.

FDA 21 CFR 177 / EC 1935:2004 required for sensors in food-contact lines. Primary position (glycol) and tertiary (utility water) are NOT food-contact. CIP return lines may require certification. KTW/WRAS provides baseline.
assessments.mitigation: Enter market via glycol-only position (non-food-contact, no certification needed); Pursue EC 1935:2004 / FDA 21 CFR 177 for wetted materials to unlock CIP position; Leverage existing KTW/WRAS drinking water approval as certification baseline

assessments.rationale: Brewery environments: cold rooms -2 to +4C, cellar 10-15C, brewhouse area up to 40C. All within standard electronics operating range.
